Defendant San Francisco Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender'S Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Oct-14-2015 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. RYAN LAPINE, ESQ. of Rosenfeld, Meyer & Susman LLP, Tel No. 310-858-7700 appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. MARIA A. CARUANA, ESQ. of Law Offices of John A. Biard, Tel No. 925-945-4491 appearing for Defendant San Francisco Lesbia, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender Pride Celebration Committee, Inc.

RULING - A R G U E D - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendant San Francisco Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender Pride Celebration Committee, Inc.'s Motion For Summary Judgment is DENIED. Defendants' reliance on Saelzler v. Advanced Group 400 (2001) 25 Cal.4th 763 is misplaced. In Saelzler, the Court held that a 28 building, 300-unit apartment owner's failure to fix broken gate locks and provide daytime security patrols was not a substantial factor in causing the sexual assault of a federal express employee who visited the complex. (Id., at p. 781.) The facial similarities to Saelzler are not compelling. Unlike Saelzler, Plaintiff Garnder's injury only occurred in a residential setting because Defendants failed to provide a barrier between their event and local dwellings. It is a well-established principle of tort liability that where several causes interact to bring about an injury it is sufficient if defendant's conduct was a "substantial factor" in causing the injury. (Nola M. v. University of Southern California (1993) 16 Cal.App.4th 421, 427.) Even though Defendant's event included annual gun violence, Defendant failed to sufficiently address a known and specific risk of injury of gun violence. It is not "pure speculation" that Defendant's total failure to (1) establish a reasonable perimeter for their event; (2) establish secured entrances; (3) require metal detectors; (4) require bag and attendee checks; (5) provide enough security guards to meet industry standards; and (6) monitor overcrowding as recommended by the San Francisco Police Department in light of annual gun violence was a substantial factor in causing Plaintiff's gunshot wound. (Thai v. Stang (1989) 214 Cal.App.3d 1264, 1276.) Plaintiff presents credible expert witness evidence that such security measures would lessen the specific probability of alcohol- and weapon-related violence occurring on the premises of Defendant's event. (Deposition of Officer Douglas McEachern 45:13-46:4;53:4-23;64:20-65:7; 70:19072:21; Deposition of Edward Cissel 96:4-22.) The causal link between Defendant's security failures and Plaintiff's injury is robust. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H.
